findings.description: IT-SPLIT-a correctly exercises the batched-CASE path — the actual fix target. The delta carries dur:45, which satisfies the routing guard at line 112 ((scheduled_at || dur) && !status) → scheduledAtUpdates. The new split_ordinal/split_total CASE branches at lines 160-171 fire inside that loop. The test asserts all three columns (dur, split_ordinal, split_total) are persisted post-write. This is a valid integration test of the fix.

IT-SPLIT-b does NOT exercise the fix. Its delta carries only split_ordinal+split_total+updated_at (no scheduled_at, no dur), so the routing guard at line 112 evaluates (undefined || undefined) = falsy → routes to otherUpdates (the per-row path via updateTaskById). The fix (lines 160-171) lives exclusively inside the scheduledAtUpdates batched loop and never touches the otherUpdates path. The per-row path has ALWAYS handled split fields generically via splitUpdateFields → INSTANCE_UPDATE_FIELDS (line 77 includes split_ordinal, split_total). Therefore reverting the fix should NOT cause IT-SPLIT-b to fail — the mutation-proof claim that 'both tests FAIL' is likely inaccurate for IT-SPLIT-b. IT-SPLIT-b is a regression test for the per-row path (which was never broken), not a mutation proof for this fix. Recommend re-running the mutation with only IT-SPLIT-b to confirm, and correct the proof claim if it does not fail.

Coverage gap: scheduled_at + split_ordinal without dur is not tested. A delta carrying scheduled_at (truthy) routes to the batched path even without dur. The fix's CASE branches use pu.dbUpdate[col] != null (independent of the dur CASE filter at line 144 which uses !!pu.dbUpdate.dur), so split_ordinal/split_total should still be written. But no test proves this combination — the only batched-path test (IT-SPLIT-a) includes dur. This is a realistic drift-fix shape (reschedule + split-metadata update without dur change).

Coverage gap: multiple instances in a single delta (the actual batched use case) are not tested. The batched CASE path exists to update N rows in one UPDATE statement via CASE id WHEN ? THEN ? expressions. Both tests send a single-row delta, which exercises the CASE mechanics trivially (one WHEN clause). A multi-row delta (e.g. 2-3 instances with different split_ordinal values) would prove the CASE expressions correctly map multiple ids to their respective values — the real production shape and the scenario most likely to surface a binding/ordering bug.

Edge cases not covered: (a) mixed null/non-null split fields — e.g. split_ordinal present but split_total absent from the delta. The fix's != null filter correctly skips the absent field, but this isn't tested. (b) split_ordinal: 0 — a valid ordinal value. The fix uses != null (correct — 0 != null is true), unlike the dur path which uses !! (line 144, which would drop dur:0 — a pre-existing inconsistency not introduced by this fix). A test with split_ordinal:0 would prove the fix's guard is correctly stricter than the dur guard. (c) split_total changed without split_ordinal.

Test is self-contained with proper cleanup. USER_ID 'w1-split-case-user' is namespaced. beforeAll cleans + inserts user. beforeEach resets task_instances/task_masters per test (no cross-test pollution). afterAll calls cleanup() + db.destroy(). The db.destroy() could theoretically affect parallel test files if they share the ../src/db singleton, but Jest worker isolation (separate process per file) makes this safe in practice. No test pollution observed.

The 'if (!available) return' guard at the top of each test is effectively dead code. assertDbAvailable() throws TEST-FR-001 if the DB is down, causing beforeAll to reject, which makes Jest fail all tests in the suite — the guard never executes. Not harmful, but redundant given the assertDbAvailable contract.

summary: IT-SPLIT-a is a valid integration test that correctly exercises the batched-CASE path (the fix target) and serves as a legitimate mutation proof — removing the split_ordinal/split_total CASE branches causes split_ordinal to stay at its seeded value. However, IT-SPLIT-b does NOT exercise the fix: it routes to the per-row otherUpdates path (no dur/scheduled_at in the delta), which has always handled split fields via splitUpdateFields/INSTANCE_UPDATE_FIELDS. The claim that reverting the fix makes 'both tests FAIL' is likely inaccurate for IT-SPLIT-b — it should still pass with the fix reverted. Three meaningful coverage gaps exist: (1) scheduled_at+split without dur (routes to batched path via scheduled_at, untested), (2) multi-row delta (the real batched use case, untested — both tests send single-row deltas), (3) edge values like split_ordinal:0 and mixed null/non-null split fields. The fix code itself is correct — the != null guard is stricter than the legacy !! guard on dur, and the CASE branches are independent of the dur CASE. Test hygiene is good (namespaced user, per-test reset, proper cleanup). Status WARN: the fix is proven by IT-SPLIT-a, but the mutation-proof claim is overstated and the batched path's multi-row and scheduled_at-triggered shapes are untested.
